claimText: The India Meteorological Department (IMD) defines a cloudburst as an event where 10 cm of rainfall is received at a station in one hour.
evidenceExcerpt: The IMD defines a cloudburst as an event where 10 cm of rainfall is received at a station in one hour.
distractorHooks: Changing 10 cm to 5 cm or 20 cm

claimText: Cloudbursts are more common in mountainous zones than in plains due to orographic lift, which forces moist air to rise, cool, and condense rapidly.
evidenceExcerpt: Cloudbursts are more common in mountainous zones due to orographic lift, where moist air is forced to rise, cooling and condensing rapidly.
examUse: Assertion-Reason question on the geographical distribution and mechanism of cloudbursts.
distractorHooks: Stating cloudbursts only occur in mountains
Attributing the phenomenon to frontal lifting instead of orographic lift

claimText: During a cloudburst, a violent upward flow of air prevents condensing raindrops from falling until the updraft weakens, causing the stored water to fall simultaneously.
evidenceExcerpt: A violent upward flow of air prevents condensing raindrops from falling until the lack of upward air causes the whole stored water to fall at once.
examUse: Statement-based question on the meteorological mechanism of cloudbursts.
distractorHooks: Stating that strong downward drafts cause the sudden release of water
Claiming horizontal winds are the primary cause of water suspension

claimText: Climate change exacerbates cloudbursts because every 1 degree Celsius rise in temperature allows the air to hold approximately 7% more moisture.
evidenceExcerpt: Climate change exacerbates cloudbursts, as every 1°C rise in temperature allows the air to hold approximately 7% more moisture.
examUse: Statement-based question linking climate change to extreme weather events.
distractorHooks: Changing 7% more moisture to decreased moisture capacity
Altering the percentage significantly to 20% or 50%
